description: The 'Defund the Police' slogan emerged during George Floyd protests in June 2020. Initial surge of support peaked at ~25-30% then declined to ~15-18% by year's end as violent crime rose, moderate Democrats distanced themselves, and the slogan proved politically toxic despite broader support for police reform.

key_events.description: George Floyd protests peak. Minneapolis City Council pledges to disband police department (June 7). 'Defund' enters mainstream discourse. Support surges among progressives.
Peak support ~28-30%. Black Lives Matter protests in 2,000+ cities. Some cities announce police budget cuts. Progressive activists push for full defunding.
Joe Biden explicitly rejects 'Defund the Police.' Jim Clyburn: 'worst slogan ever.' Democratic establishment distances itself. Police unions launch counter-campaign.
Summer crime surge in major cities. Homicides up 30%+ in Chicago, NYC, Philadelphia. Republicans weaponize 'defund' in campaign ads. Minneapolis council backtracks.
Post-election analysis: Democrats blame 'defund' for House seat losses. Abigail Spanberger: 'we need to not ever use the word socialist or defund.' Movement retreats to activist circles.

calibration_notes: Classic slogan overreach pattern: peaked rapidly then declined as political backlash mounted. USA Today/Ipsos (June 2020): 25% support 'defund,' but 60%+ support police reform. The gap between slogan support and policy support was the key dynamic. Gallup showed desire for police presence in Black communities remained at 61%. Tests: framing effects (slogan vs. policy), elite counter-mobilization, crime as counter-narrative, social desirability bias decay.
